caption: Figure 1: Comparison between conventional single-disorder assessment based on interview- or reading-based paradigms and our multi-disorder differential diagnosis setting built on a psychology-inspired multimodal elicitation paradigm, together with the proposed paradigm-aware multimodal learning frame- work. MD, ANX, SC and HC refer to depression, anxiety, schizophrenia and healthy controls, respectively.
description2: The figure compares conventional single-disorder assessments based on interview or reading paradigms with a multi-disorder differential diagnosis setting using a psychology-inspired multimodal elicitation paradigm.
description3: A multi-panel plot contrasts conventional single-disorder assessment with a multimodal elicitation paradigm for differential diagnosis across depression, anxiety, schizophrenia, and healthy controls.
questions: How does the new approach contrast with conventional, reading-based assessment paradigms?
What is the role of psychology-inspired multimodal elicitation in this framework?

caption: Figure 2: Overview of the psychology-inspired multimodal stimulus paradigm.
description2: The figure presents an overview of a psychology-inspired multimodal stimulus paradigm.
description3: Two-panel plot displays a multimodal stimulus paradigm, showing five tasks connected by arrows and accompanied by text and audio examples.
questions: What is the general approach to stimulus presentation in this study?
How are different sensory inputs combined to create the overall experimental paradigm?

caption: Figure 3: The overview of the proposed paradigm-level prompt-guided learning framework, taking the four-class downstream task as an example. Stage 1 pretrains a video feature extractor by aligning visual cues with paradigm-level semantic descriptions generated by a MLLM. Stage 2 integrates these paradigm-aware visual features with complementary audio and text modalities through a cross-modality interaction module for multi-disorder detection.
description2: The diagram illustrates the prompt-guided learning framework, comprising two stages: pretraining a video feature extractor and integrating visual cues with audio and text modalities.
description3: A multi-panel plot outlines a prompt-guided learning framework, with stages for pretraining and multimodal joint training.
questions: How does the framework leverage descriptions to improve video feature extraction?
What are the main stages involved in the proposed learning framework?

caption: Figure 4: Modality-level ablation study.
description2: A modality-level ablation study is plotted or structured in this figure.
description3: Four-panel line chart displays multiple metrics (ACC, Macro-P, Macro-R, Macro-F1, Weighted-F1) across six categories (V, T, A, VT, AT, AV, AVT) on the x-axis.
questions: What is the primary focus of this modality-level ablation study?
How do the experimental conditions affect the overall study results?

caption: Figure 5: Module-level ablation study on the paradigm-aware multimodal learning framework. (PT: Pretraining; CA: Cross- Attention; CL: Contrastive Learning) ’w/o’ denotes ’without’.
description2: A module-level ablation study is plotted, featuring components such as pretraining, cross-attention, and contrastive learning in a workflow diagram.
description3: Four-panel radar chart displays module ablation study results, showing performance across ACC, Macro-P, Macro-R, and Macro-F1 for different configurations.
questions: How does removing different components affect the overall performance of the learning framework?
What is the relative impact of different modules on the multimodal learning system's performance?

caption: Figure 6: t-SNE visualization for different diagnosis tasks. Left shows the Baseline, and the right shows Ours.
description2: A two-panel plot displays a t-SNE visualization of data for different diagnosis tasks, with the left panel representing the Baseline and the right panel representing Ours.
description3: Four-panel t-SNE visualizations, arranged in a 2x2 grid, display scatter plots of data points colored by diagnosis categories (MD, SC, ANX, Health).
questions: How do the Baseline and Ours approaches visually represent different diagnosis tasks?
What is the general organization of data points shown on the left versus the right?
